assessor_1_raw:
ASSESSOR 1 — real_world_framing_008

The vendor's AUROC = 0.85 and sensitivity = 0.88 are strong metrics for sepsis prediction. The vendor's claim of readiness for immediate deployment 'across all acute care settings' is not supported by the evidence for three reasons.

First, CAH out-of-distribution: the validation was performed at four academic medical centers. Critical access hospitals operate with fundamentally different patient populations, acuity distributions, staffing ratios, and workflows. There is no CAH-specific validation data. The alert burden at CAH patient acuity levels may differ materially from the 4.2 alerts per 100 patient-days observed at academic centers.

Second, alert fatigue: the five CAHs in this network have a documented >60% dismiss-without-action rate for existing clinical decision support alerts — a formally reported patient safety concern. Adding a high-sensitivity sepsis alert into this environment will predictably result in the sepsis alerts being dismissed along with the existing noise. The intended clinical benefit will not materialize.

Third, workflow actionability gap: CAHs lack dedicated intensivists, and nurses manage 4–6 patients simultaneously. The clinical response to a sepsis alert — IV access, lactate draw, blood cultures, antibiotics within 1–3 hours — requires sustained attention and resources that CAH staffing may not support. An alert that triggers a required response that cannot be executed creates liability without clinical benefit.

Verdict: Network-wide deployment including CAHs is inappropriate. Phased deployment — academic and community hospitals first, CAH-specific pilot before CAH contract — is required.
assessor_2_raw:
ASSESSOR 2 — real_world_framing_008

Three independent reasons CAH deployment is inappropriate: (1) no CAH validation — academic-center AUROC does not transfer to CAH setting without evidence; (2) >60% documented alert dismiss rate makes additional alert system counterproductive in CAH context; (3) sepsis bundle execution feasibility not assessed for CAH staffing constraints. Network-wide contract should exclude CAHs pending specific validation and pilot.
assessor_3_raw:
ASSESSOR 3 — real_world_framing_008

CAH-specific concerns: (1) OOD — no CAH validation data; (2) documented alert fatigue means additional alerts will be dismissed; (3) sepsis bundle actionability gap in understaffed setting. Model quality is strong for academic settings. CAH deployment requires separate validation and workflow assessment before contract inclusion.
synthesis_raw:
SYNTHESIS — real_world_framing_008 (Ensemble, Run 1)

All three assessors identify the same three issues: CAH out-of-distribution validation gap, alert fatigue context, and workflow actionability gap. The model quality is not disputed; the deployment scope is the problem.

EMPIRICAL TEST SPECIFICATION: Prospective 90-day pilot at one CAH before any CAH deployment contract is signed. Measure: alert volume per nursing FTE vs. academic center baseline, actionability rate (% of alerts with documented clinical response), and time-to-sepsis-bundle initiation vs. pre-deployment baseline. Supports critique if actionability rate < 30%. Supports defense if actionability rate ≥ 60% and time-to-antibiotic is reduced.

empirical_test.condition: 90-day CAH pilot measuring alert actionability rate and time-to-sepsis-bundle initiation before any CAH deployment contract is signed.
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Alert actionability rate < 30% or time-to-bundle not reduced vs. baseline.
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Actionability rate ≥ 60% and measurable reduction in time-to-antibiotic.
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Moderate actionability (40–60%) concentrated in day-shift, suggesting staffing interaction.
